data.title: Moog forecasts FY 2026 adjusted EPS $10.60 ±$0.20 as tariff pressure rises to 110 bps
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Moog Inc. (MOG.A) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered an outstanding second quarter" (President, CEO & Director Patrick Roche), highlighting "double-digit revenue growth," "records for both total and 12-month backlog with 12-month backlog up 33% from the prior year," and "record adjusted earnings per share" tied to "improved adjusted operating margin."
  • Roche said defense demand is being shaped by geopolitics: "The Middle Eastern war has further increased the need and urgency to boost U.S. defense industrial manufacturing capacity," adding that "production rates on key missile defense programs are anticipated to increase by factors ranging from 2 to 4x over the next few years."
  • Roche described portfolio actions and capacity moves, including: "This quarter, we exited the general aviation avionics market with the licensing of IP and a last-time buy for our customers," and progress on commercial aircraft balance-sheet initiatives, including "inventory destocking exceeding our plan" and selecting "a fourth-party logistics coordinator who will assume the management of nearly 30% of our suppliers."
  • "Sales in the second quarter of $1.1 billion were 13% higher than last year's second quarter" (Executive VP & CFO Jennifer Walter), alongside "adjusted operating margin in the second quarter was 13.4%" and "nearly $100 million of free cash flow."

Outlook

  • "We've updated our guidance for fiscal '26" (President, CEO & Director Roche), saying the company "increased sales and adjusted diluted earnings per share and held adjusted operating margin and free cash flow conversion unchanged."
  • "We're now expecting 110 basis points of pressure from tariffs in FY '26, up 30 basis points from our previous guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Walter), attributing the change to mix: "Increased activity within our Industrial segment is causing this additional pressure."
  • "We're increasing our FY '26 adjusted earnings per share guidance by $0.40 to $10.60, plus or minus $0.20" (Executive VP & CFO Walter), and "for the third quarter, we're forecasting earnings per share to be $2.65, plus or minus $0.10."
  • Segment guide changes were framed as demand-driven in defense and industrial, and timing-driven in commercial aircraft: "For Commercial Aircraft, we're decreasing our sales guidance by $20 million" (Executive VP & CFO Walter) due to "our decision to slow the rate of incoming inventory on certain narrow-body platforms."

Financial Results

  • "Sales were $314 million" in Space & Defense, "up 16%" year over year (Executive VP & CFO Walter), with demand "particularly strong for space vehicles and missile controls."
  • Commercial Aircraft sales were "$247 million" (up "15%"), Military Aircraft sales were "$235 million" (up "10%"), and Industrial sales were "$256 million" (up "9%") (Executive VP & CFO Walter), with Industrial growth linked to "the expanding data center cooling market" and FX.
  • Profitability commentary centered on tariffs and segment execution: "We achieved these results despite 100 basis points of pressure from tariffs" in Q2 (Executive VP & CFO Walter), while company adjusted operating margin was "13.4%, up 90 basis points" year over year.
  • Capital structure and liquidity actions were emphasized: "we amended our $1.1 billion revolving credit facility and our $250 million term loan," issued "$500 million of 5.5% senior notes" and used proceeds to call notes (Executive VP & CFO Walter); leverage ended Q2 at "1.8x."

Q&A

  • Jonathan Tanwanteng, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked for an update on missile-controls growth and ability to ramp faster; President, CEO & Director Roche pointed to primes’ multi-year ramp and said PAC-3 output could rise "from 650 missiles per year" to "2,000 per year," adding Moog has "facility space available and the capabilities to increase our rate at the level that's being asked for."
  • Jonathan Tanwanteng, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked about war impact on commercial aircraft demand; President, CEO & Director Roche said Moog shut down "2 forward stocking locations in the Middle East" and expects a shift to "more fuel-efficient aircraft," adding aftermarket demand is expected to sustain "roughly the rate -- our rough run rate" and "it's in the guidance."
  • Kristine Liwag: asked how Moog capitalizes on defense growth and whether customers help fund capacity; President, CEO & Director Roche said missile delivery performance has been "100% on time, 100% quality" and described a "partial takeaway from one of our competitors," while also noting Moog is "prepared to invest" and is discussing "how we support that ramp" with customers and government.
  • Gautam Khanna, TD Cowen, Research Division: pressed on the Commercial Aircraft guidance cut; Executive VP & CFO Walter said it is "a timing thing" driven by choosing "not bringing in materials ahead of when we need it," and confirmed it is "by Moog" rather than OEM demand.
  • Gautam Khanna, TD Cowen, Research Division: asked why full-year tariff pressure rose; President, CEO & Director Roche called tariffs "a pretty fluid situation" and tied the change to Industrial volume that is "particularly sensitive to the tariffs."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive but probing on capacity, tariffs, and commercial-aircraft timing, including repeated efforts to clarify what was demand-driven vs. Moog-driven, such as "Timing by Moog, not by the OEM customer" (Gautam Khanna, TD Cowen, Research Division).
  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks ("Demand is strong" and "we're delivering ahead of our guidance") and more explanatory in Q&A, repeatedly pointing to actions already underway (facility capacity, supplier changes, inventory pacing) and saying "we have reflected our current thinking into the guidance" (President, CEO & Director Roche).
  • Compared with Q1, Q2 carried more explicit discussion of the Middle Eastern war’s operational implications and greater emphasis on tariff variability, while maintaining a similar confidence posture around defense demand and operational execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance direction remained consistent: both quarters raised FY 2026 sales and adjusted EPS while holding adjusted operating margin and free cash flow conversion unchanged; Q2 added a higher tariff-pressure assumption ("110 basis points" vs. "up 30 basis points from our previous guidance") (Executive VP & CFO Walter).
  • Commercial Aircraft narrative shifted from Q1’s emphasis on production ramps and aftermarket strength to Q2’s explicit decision to slow inbound material on certain narrow-body platforms to manage inventory timing (Executive VP & CFO Walter).
  • Defense discussion evolved from Q1’s broad strength and capacity urgency to Q2’s more explicit ramp language ("2 to 4x") and program-level production examples (PAC-3 volumes) tied to the war-driven procurement environment (President, CEO & Director Roche).

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs remained a core headwind: "We're now expecting 110 basis points of pressure from tariffs in FY '26" (Executive VP & CFO Walter), with mitigation described via pricing and supply-chain actions, while Roche said the situation remains "pretty fluid."
  • Commercial Aircraft working-capital execution remained a focus: Walter said, "it has taken us longer" to resolve "existing operational challenges" tied to inventories, while pointing to progress in "rescheduling material receipts" and expecting next quarter free cash flow conversion at "about 100%."
  • War-related disruptions were operational and cost-related rather than demand-collapse oriented in management’s framing, including shutdown of Middle East stocking locations and higher fuel/transportation costs in the Philippines (President, CEO & Director Roche).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as an execution-driven quarter with higher sales, record adjusted EPS, and strong free cash flow, then raised FY 2026 adjusted EPS guidance to $10.60 ±$0.20 while keeping operating margin and free cash flow conversion targets intact despite higher expected tariff pressure. The call emphasized accelerating defense demand and capacity readiness (including missile program ramps and facility investments), while the Commercial Aircraft guide reduction was positioned as a deliberate, Moog-driven move to slow incoming inventory timing rather than a change in end-market demand.

data.title: Byline outlines $99M-$101M Q2 net interest income range as it targets mid-single-digit loan growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Byline Bancorp (BY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Roberto Herencia framed the operating backdrop around “shifting interest rate expectations, inconsistent economic signals, policy uncertainty and heightened geopolitical tensions with the Iran war at the center of it,” and said Byline remains “focused on driving value for our holders as we work and make progress... toward becoming the preeminent commercial bank in Chicago.”
  • President Alberto Paracchini reported Q1 profitability and capital actions, including net income of $37.6 million and EPS of $0.83, revenue of $112.4 million, net interest income of $99.9 million, and NIM of 4.33%. He also highlighted balance sheet and capital metrics including deposits of $7.8 billion, expenses of $57 million, ACL at 1.46% of total loans, CET1 “over 12.5%,” and “returned 40% of net income back to shareholders by repurchasing approximately 318,000 shares... in addition to our quarterly dividend of $0.12 per share.”
  • CFO Thomas J. Bell said loan pipelines “remain strong,” and reiterated loan growth expectations: “we expect full year loan growth in the mid-single digits.” He added that deposit costs improved to 1.91% and described Q1 net interest income drivers, including “2 fewer days in the quarter,” lower earning-asset yields, and “higher borrowing costs as a result of a balance sheet hedge that matured in March.”

Outlook

  • CFO Thomas J. Bell set near-term net interest income parameters: “this implies a net interest income range of $99 million to $101 million in the second quarter,” and said, “we expect net interest income to grow driven by overall balance sheet growth and disciplined deposit pricing in the event short-term rates move lower.”
  • CFO Bell guided Q2 noninterest income: “we expect gain on sale to average $5.5 million per quarter and our noninterest income to be in the $14 million to $15 million range for the second quarter,” while keeping cost guidance unchanged: “our noninterest expense full-year guidance remains unchanged at $58 million to $60 million per quarter.”
  • On rates embedded in planning, CFO Bell said, “our outlook for net interest income is based on the forward curve, which currently assumes no rate cuts or hikes in 2026,” compared with the prior quarter’s assumption of a 50 basis point decline in the Fed funds rate for 2026.

Financial Results

  • President Alberto Paracchini reported profitability and operating metrics including ROA of 156 basis points, ROTCE of 13.77%, pretax pre-provision income of $55.2 million, and an efficiency ratio of 49.8%, calling it “one of the lowest levels we've reported since becoming a public company.”
  • CFO Thomas J. Bell detailed NII and margin performance: “net interest income was $99.9 million in Q1, down 1% from the prior quarter and up 13% year-over-year,” and “the net interest margin was stable at 4.33%, declining modestly by 2 basis points from the last quarter.”
  • CFO Bell attributed the Q1 noninterest income decline to valuation marks: “noninterest income totaled $12.5 million in Q1, which was down approximately $3.2 million linked quarter,” including “an additional negative fair value mark on loan servicing assets of $755,000 and a $1.3 million decline in fair value of equity securities.”

Q&A

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Asked about origination/payoff drivers and whether declines were macro/seasonal; Alberto Paracchini: said business activity was “pretty good,” and explained payoffs were largely “recycling loan participations and loans that we had acquired,” adding that “if you strip that out, I think loan growth would have been somewhere in the 4% kind of level for the quarter.”
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Asked about loan-yield trajectory and roll-off vs new production; Thomas J. Bell: “Roll-offs are... $300 million-ish like a 450 kind of coupon, so new production is typically around 675, 680 kind of coupons.”
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Asked about M&A activity and sellers; Alberto Paracchini: said uncertainty “maybe causing some sellers to pause,” but “the underlying level of conversations continues to be... pretty healthy.”
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: Asked about buyback capacity; Thomas J. Bell: “we have plenty of room to continue to repurchase shares.”
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: Asked why grow CDs amid limited loan growth and how competition looks; Thomas J. Bell: said CDs provide diversification and funding, with “the opportunity to potentially cross-sell those CD customers and other products.”
  • Damon DelMonte, KBW: Asked what’s building the loan pipeline; Alberto Paracchini: said “all segments,” with real estate more rate-sensitive and “pipelines are solid” in commercial and leasing, also noting sponsors and transaction activity.
  • Damon DelMonte, KBW: Asked about securities portfolio path; Thomas J. Bell: “stable... we'll probably reinvest cash flows,” and Alberto Paracchini said deposit growth that outpaces loans would “end up in the securities portfolio.”
  • Brandon Rud, Stephens: Asked about deposit-cost trajectory and Q2 starting point; Thomas J. Bell: “March was exactly on top of where the cost of funds was for the quarter,” and added the CD book is “4 months, 5 months.”
  • Brandon Rud, Stephens: Asked whether Byline will manage below $10 billion and Durbin impact; Alberto Paracchini: “we are not trying to manage the balance sheet artificially stay under $10 billion”; Thomas J. Bell: “about 4 basis points to ROA as a decline,” and “$3.5 million to $4 million” annually starting “July 1 of '27.”
  • Brian Martin, Brean Capital: Asked if deposit costs should be stable and about competitive pressures; Thomas J. Bell: “relatively flat, maybe down a little bit,” and said “I don't think anything is crazy at this point.”
  • Brian Martin, Brean Capital: Asked about commercial payments contribution timing; Thomas J. Bell: “we'll start seeing that more here in the second half of the year.”
  • Brian Martin, Brean Capital: Asked for a fee-income baseline into Q2 after Q1 “noise”; Thomas J. Bell: reiterated “$14 million to $15 million,” citing “lower swap fee income” expected to “pick up,” and “a small lower valuation on the sale of some lease assets... a one-off.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: Slightly negative to neutral in tone, pressing on loan payoffs vs production, deposit-cost trajectory, securities growth, and the $10 billion/Durbin implications, including “why you opted to grow CDs as much as you did” (Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group) and questions on whether costs are “relatively stable” given competition (Brian Martin, Brean Capital).
  • Management: Slightly positive and steady, repeatedly emphasizing stability and planned execution, including “we were pleased with our results” (President Alberto Paracchini) and “we expect full year loan growth in the mid-single digits” (CFO Thomas J. Bell), while addressing uncertainty with “disciplined execution and long-term thinking” (CEO Roberto Herencia).
  • Versus last quarter: The prepared-remarks tone stayed upbeat in both quarters, but Q1 carried more explicit macro/geopolitical framing from the CEO, while Q4 centered on shareholder returns and regulatory preparation; analyst questioning remained focused on balance sheet dynamics and capital, with Q1 adding more emphasis on crossing $10 billion and Durbin timing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management’s rate backdrop embedded in guidance shifted from “assumes 50 basis point decline... for 2026” (Q4) to “assumes no rate cuts or hikes in 2026” (Q1), while Q2 NII guidance was provided at $99 million to $101 million.
  • Net interest margin moved from 4.35% in Q4 to 4.33% in Q1, while deposits increased from $7.6 billion in Q4 to $7.8 billion in Q1, and loans were described as “down slightly” in Q1 after Q4 loan growth.
  • Capital return messaging evolved from a new authorization “to buy back up to 5% of outstanding shares” and a dividend increase (Q4) to Q1 execution, with “repurchasing approximately 318,000 shares... in addition to our quarterly dividend of $0.12 per share.”

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Roberto Herencia cited “policy uncertainty” and “heightened geopolitical tensions with the Iran war at the center of it,” positioning Byline’s response as “disciplined execution and long-term thinking.”
  • Management highlighted loan payoffs and acquired-loan runoff as a key balance-sheet dynamic; Alberto Paracchini characterized runoff as “planned,” tied to participations and acquired books.
  • Management and analysts focused on the $10 billion asset threshold and Durbin; management said it is not “trying to manage the balance sheet artificially stay under $10 billion,” and quantified Durbin as “$3.5 million to $4 million” annually starting July 1, 2027.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a “solid start to the year,” anchored by a 4.33% NIM, stable credit metrics, and lower expenses, while keeping full-year loan growth expectations at “mid-single digits.” The forward outlook centered on CFO guidance for Q2 net interest income of $99 million to $101 million, Q2 noninterest income of $14 million to $15 million, and unchanged expense guidance of $58 million to $60 million per quarter, alongside continued capital flexibility as the bank approaches crossing $10 billion in assets and prepares for a Durbin impact beginning in mid-2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: FactSet Research Systems dividend preview: May raise to continue 26-year run?
data.description:
FactSet Research Systems (FDS) is expected to continue its 26-year streak of increasing dividends. Analysts expect a consensus FY26 dividend of ~$4.48 per share, which means a quarterly payout of ~$1.12 per share.

The company had already reported its Q2 FY26 results on March 31, so this expected dividend increase comes after its latest earnings update.

This would be a small ~1.8% increase from the current dividend of ~$1.10 per quarter. The company last declared this $1.10 dividend in February, with an annual dividend yield of ~1.93%.

Last year as well, the company increased its dividend by ~5.8%, from $1.04 to $1.10 per share at the end of May.

Over the last 5 years, the company has grown its dividend at an average rate of ~7.39%, while keeping an average payout ratio of ~25.45%.

In terms of dividend quality, Research Systems (FDS) scores an A rating on safety and consistency, a B+ on growth, and a D+ on yield.

data.title: There is more room for Intel to run – KKM Financial CEO
data.description:
Jeff Kilburg, founder and CEO of KKM Financial, is holding firm on his Intel (INTC) position despite the stock’s remarkable surge, declaring that the chipmaker still has significant upside potential.

In an interview with CNBC, Kilburg noted that Intel has risen over 100% following an 85% gain the previous year, yet he sees no reason to take profits.

“The signal is not yet,” Kilburg said when asked about his exit strategy.

Intel (INTC) shares jumped to as much as $85—currently +22%—after its Q1 results delivered a beat, and now its second-quarter revenue outlook is expected to surpass what analysts anticipated. 

Kilburg views Intel (INTC) as an “essential name” critical to the U.S. economy and American way of life, a conviction he has held for years despite skepticism.

“I got laughed at for saying that three years ago on air, but it is essential,” he explained. The KKM Financial CEO emphasized that momentum remains the driving force behind his bullish outlook, stating simply, “I want to own it.”

The investment case has strengthened considerably thanks to Intel’s (INTC) foundry business turnaround, according to Kilburg. The division, which previously generated no profit, is expected to produce $3B next year and now serves as a template for the company’s future growth.

Kilburg pointed to the “TerraFab” development as the catalyst that fundamentally changed the company’s trajectory.

While acknowledging that widespread media attention on Intel (INTC) makes him “a little nervous,” Kilburg contrasted the current enthusiasm with the deep pessimism when the stock traded under $20 and the U.S. government acquired its stake.

His firm plans to rebalance the position in their Essential 40 ETF (ESN) when appropriate, but he emphasized, “I don’t see the momentum stopping.”

Kilburg described Intel as “a workhorse” that has emerged from the volatility of 2022 and 2023.

Beyond Intel, Kilburg discussed the “dispersion” within the Mag Seven tech stocks (MAGS), noting opportunities to add positions in Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL) during sentiment-driven selloffs.

He characterized the current environment as a “SaaS apocalypse” affecting software names (IGV), (XSW), (IGPT) while semiconductor stocks (SMH), (SOXX), (SOXL) continue their strong performance.

On the broader AI arms race, Kilburg expressed optimism about companies demonstrating strong returns on their capital expenditure, praising Amazon’s (AMZN) approach where “every dollar they’re spending, that ROI is being matched.”

Tech ETFs: (VGT), (XLK), (IYW), (FTEC), (IXN), and (RSPT).

Semiconductor ETFs: (SMH), (SOXX), (SOXL), (FTXL), (XSD), (USD), (PSI), and (SEMI).

Software ETFs: (IGV), (XSW), (IGPT), (AOTS)

data.title: Covenant Logistics sees $7,000 to $10,000 average truck cost increase next year while signaling sequential improvement in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Covenant Logistics Group (CVLG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Our first quarter was unique in that it included 2 of the worst and one of the best months we have experienced in the last 3 years." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer James Grant)
  • "The trajectory was positive and has continued into April, leaving us with conviction that the change in the market is structural, not seasonal." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)
  • "Consolidated freight revenue increased by 15.9% or approximately $38.7 million to $281.9 million" and "consolidated adjusted operating income shrank by 11.5% to $9.6 million" as Expedited faced "severe weather" and "higher net fuel costs." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)
  • "Our net indebtedness as of March 31 decreased by approximately $51 million to $245.3 million" with "an adjusted leverage ratio of approximately 1.8x and debt-to-capital ratio of 37.6%." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)
  • "The Expedited segment reported an adjusted operating ratio of 99.1% for the quarter" and management said, "Going forward, we have line of sight to sequential improvement in this segment throughout the year." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)
  • "Dedicated's 95.5% adjusted operating ratio was an improvement" and management said its goal is to "grow the fleet serving high service niches" and "reduce the fleet that is exposed to more commoditized end markets where returns are inadequate." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)

Outlook

  • "We believe 2026 will be known as a transition year in the freight market with sequential incremental financial improvement to occur each quarter." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer James Grant)
  • "During the first quarter, we secured rate and lane improvements with existing customers and developed a mature pipeline of new customers with attractive pricing on a level that has not occurred since 2022." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)
  • "The nature of these bids is the new rates and lanes take effect a few weeks after being negotiated" and "the first quarter activity will begin to show up in the second quarter." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing that "the improvements are likely to come later in the year," management’s current call emphasized immediacy: "Revenue trends during the first 3 weeks of April remained strong across all of our business units." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant; Q4 2025: Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)

Financial Results

  • "Consolidated freight revenue increased by 15.9%... to $281.9 million" while "consolidated adjusted operating income shrank by 11.5% to $9.6 million." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer James Grant)
  • Balance sheet leverage moved lower in-quarter: "net indebtedness... decreased... to $245.3 million" and management attributed it to "selling a significant amount of used equipment in the quarter and buying very little new equipment." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)
  • Management flagged near-term leverage variability: "With equipment deliveries concentrated in the last 3 quarters, leverage ratio may increase modestly in the next couple of quarters depending on the timing of deliveries and the prices for used equipment." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)
  • Segment profitability and investment returns were framed as a key watchpoint: "On an adjusted basis, return on average invested capital was 5% for the trailing 4 quarters versus 7.6% for the same period in the prior year." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)

Q&A

  • Jason Seidl, TD Cowen: asked about poultry/dedicated and DoD trends; President Bunn responded, "we're really happy with our pipeline, poultry and non-poultry" and on DoD, "that business was pretty good in February, better in March and better in April than it was in March." (President M. Bunn)
  • Seidl, TD Cowen: asked about early peak-season capacity discussions; President Bunn said, "we haven't gotten as far as talking about peak now" but "we're seeing more people want to talk about dedicated capacity on the team side than we've seen since '21 or '22." (President Bunn)
  • Seidl, TD Cowen: asked about driver pay pressure; President Bunn said, "there's no doubt you're talking something in that mid-single digits probably on driver pay, maybe high single digits if this thing gets really hot." (President Bunn)
  • Jeffrey Kauffman, Vertical Research Partners: asked what could go right/wrong; CEO Parker said, "it is absolutely turning around" and cited enforcement-driven tightening: "somewhere around 2% to 3% of capacity has been eliminated." (Founder, CEO & Chairman David Parker)
  • Kauffman, Vertical Research Partners: asked about how much rate gains are absorbed by wages/inflation; President Bunn said, "driver pay is 30% of maybe total cost" and "you'll probably end up netting 60% to 70% maybe of bottom line without other inflation items." (President Bunn)
  • Kauffman, Vertical Research Partners: asked about truck pricing and tariffs; CFO Grant responded, "we do have pricing for next year" and "we are looking at probably a $7,000 to $10,000 probably cost increase" for next year’s purchases; President Bunn added, "those are not tariff-related increases" and are "because of emissions." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant; President Bunn)
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: asked about Washington policy and tort reform; CEO Parker said, "we've gone from a 0% chance to -- my number is 25% chance" on tort reform and added, "I believe Dalilah's law will pass." (Founder, CEO & Chairman Parker)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive and forward-leaning, with questions centered on tightening capacity, pay inflation, and policy; one example was Kauffman’s framing that "Everybody is starting to talk about positive things for the first time in about 3 years." (Jeffrey Kauffman, Vertical Research Partners)
  • Management’s tone was positive in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with explicit confidence language such as "we have line of sight" and "it is absolutely turning around." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer James Grant; Founder, CEO & Chairman David Parker)
  • Versus the prior quarter’s optimism that was conditioned on timing ("The improvements are likely to come later in the year"), the current call used more immediate, conviction-oriented language ("structural, not seasonal" and "continued into April"). (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer James Grant)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 management emphasized acceleration and April traction: "Revenue trends during the first 3 weeks of April remained strong" and "the change in the market is structural, not seasonal." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer James Grant)
  • Q4 2025 focused more on capital plan and integration, including "only $40 million to $50 million of net CapEx for the year" and that Star was expected to be "accretive to earnings during the first half of 2026." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer James Grant)
  • Expedited narrative shifted from underperformance but manageable (Q4: "97.2%" adjusted OR with shutdown impacts) to a sharper disappointment in Q1 ("99.1%... fell well short of our expectations") while still reiterating sequential improvement. (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer James Grant)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Severe weather and rising fuel costs adversely impacted" Expedited, and management linked performance sensitivity to utilization: the operation is "requiring high utilization to cover the fixed cost." (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer James Grant)
  • Management warned of equipment and market timing risk: "leverage ratio may increase modestly" near term, and new equipment cost inflation was quantified as "$7,000 to $10,000" on average for next year. (Executive VP, CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer Grant)
  • CEO Parker highlighted macro/geopolitical uncertainty: "What could go wrong? I want the war to get over with because capacity is increasing" and he discussed oil sensitivity to consumer spending. (Founder, CEO & Chairman David Parker)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a weather- and fuel-challenged quarter that pressured Expedited profitability, but repeatedly pointed to improving trends into April, a strengthening new-business pipeline, and expectations for "sequential incremental financial improvement" through 2026. Leadership also highlighted balance sheet deleveraging driven by used-equipment sales, while flagging upcoming cost inflation in new truck purchases and potential mid- to high-single-digit driver pay pressure as capacity tightens.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Top 10 ETFs with largest P&G exposure ranked by SA Quant rating
data.description:
Procter & Gamble's (PG) solid fiscal Q3 beat sent shares up 3.3% on Friday, with organic sales of 3% topping estimates and non-GAAP EPS of $1.59 clearing the $1.56 consensus.

In light of this, below is a list of the top 10 ETFs with the largest exposure to Procter & Gamble, ranked according to their Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings. The list primarily features funds within the consumer staples sector, evaluated based on quantitative metrics including market capitalization, recent price performance, and other key measures.

The list is topped by Invesco Dow Jones Industrial Average Div ETF (DJD), with a Quant Rating of 2.84, followed by iShares S&P 500 Consumer Staples Sector UCITS ETF (IMSBF) at 2.74.

Despite being the highest-rated funds in this group, the ratings fall within the “Hold” to “Strong Sell” categories. Vanguard Consumer Staples Index Fund ETF (VDC) and Fidelity MSCI Consumer Staples Index ETF (FSTA) both sit at the threshold between “Hold” and “Sell” with ratings of 2.50. The lowest-ranked fund on the list is StSt SPDR S&P US Consumer Staples Select Sector UCITS ETF (SECTF), with a Strong Sell rating of 1.40.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings system grades securities based on their relative performance on critical quantitative measures, including valuation, growth, stock momentum, and profitability. Ratings are given on a scale from 1 to 5, with any rating of 3.5 or above considered bullish and any rating of 2.5 or below considered bearish.

Here is the list:

  1. Invesco Dow Jones Industrial Average Div ETF (DJD), Quant Rating: 2.84

  2. iShares S&P 500 Consumer Staples Sector UCITS ETF USD A (IMSBF), Quant Rating: 2.74

  3. Vanguard Consumer Staples Index Fund ETF (VDC), Quant Rating: 2.50

  4. Fidelity MSCI Consumer Staples Index ETF (FSTA), Quant Rating: 2.50

  5. State Street Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP), Quant Rating: 2.42

  6. FT Vest DJIA Dogs 10 Target Income ETF (DOGG), Quant Rating: 2.28

  7. iShares Global Consumer Staples ETF (KXI), Quant Rating: 2.22

  8. ProShares Ultra Consumer Staples ETF (UGE), Quant Rating: 2.09

  9. iShares US Consumer Staples ETF (IYK), Quant Rating: 2.08

  10. StSt SPDR S&P US Consumer Staples Select Sector UCITS ETF Acc (SECTF), Quant Rating: 1.40

data.title: BridgeBio gains amid report of settlement in Pfizer Vyndamax patent dispute
data.description:
BridgeBio (BBIO) rose 2.9% after a court filing appeared to indicate a settlement in a generic patent case over Pfizer's (PFE) Vyndamax drug.

Pfizer (PFE) and generic drug maker Dexcel agreed to stipulate and for a dismissal in a patent lawsuit over Pfizer's Vyndamax/tafamidis drug, according to a court filing on Friday. Two other generic drug makers are also defendants in the patent case, which is set for next week in Delaware.

"For BBIO shares, we think investors would view settlement between PFE with Dexcel (lead generic defendant) as a step in the right direction, though we note further details have not yet been released (including settlement date + potential agreements with other defendants)," Barclays analyst Eliana Merle wrote in a note on Friday.

Barclays sees base case settlement outcome could be a generic Vyndamax entry in 2031. BridgeBio (BBIO) makes a drug similar to Vyndamax/tafamidis called Attruby for a deadly form of heart disease.

"We think BBIO shares could be up ~30-40% (PFE settles/wins scenario) vs down ~20-30% (PFE loses scenario); though we think regardless of outcome this could still be a clearing event and shares could recover from there," Merle, who has an overweight rating and $157 price target on BBIO, added in the note.

data.title: Humacyte regains ex-U.S. rights to Symvess from Fresenius Medical Care
data.description:
Humacyte (HUMA) announced on Friday that the company regained the exclusive rights to develop and commercialize its lead product, Symvess, outside the U.S. following an amendment to a 2018 agreement with Fresenius Medical Care (FMS).

Under the distribution agreement signed on June 25, 2018, Humacyte (HUMA) granted Fresenius (FMS) ex-U.S. rights to develop and commercialize Symvess, which the FDA approved in 2024 for extremity vascular trauma.

The Durham, North Carolina-based biotech said that as part of an amendment added to the agreement on April 21, it has regained those rights from Fresenius (FMS). The amended deal allows it to develop, commercialize, and handle all regulatory matters related to Symvess outside the U.S.

In exchange, Humacyte (HUMA) has agreed to pay the German company low-single-digit royalties on overseas net sales of Symvess following a two-year royalty-free period. The prior terms of the deal in relation to the U.S. rights of Symvess remain unchanged.

data.title: Middle East conflict to add over 700 business insolvency cases in U.S. - Allianz
data.description:
The Middle East conflict is expected to add over 700 business insolvency cases in the U.S. in 2026, and over 200 cases in 2027, according to a report by Allianz Research.

The crisis is expected to make 2026 the fifth consecutive year of rising global business insolvencies.

U.S. is expected to extend its rise in insolvencies to +9% in 2026 from +7% in 2025.

The extended rise in business insolvencies is estimated to put 2.2M jobs directly at risk globally in 2026, followed by a marginal decrease in 2027 of 34K.

Related ETFs: (USMC), (USA), (USPX), (USMV), (USMF), (USVM), (USHY), (USFR), (USIG), (UST).

data.title: P&G beats Q3 and rises 3%, but the valuation grade says it's still expensive at a D rating
data.description:
Procter & Gamble's (PG) solid fiscal Q3 beat sent shares up 3.3% on Friday, with organic sales of 3% topping estimates and non-GAAP EPS of $1.59 clearing the $1.56 consensus.

Despite the strong quarter, the household products giant carries a D valuation grade, placing it firmly in Sell territory relative to its fundamentals, and ranking fifth out of eight stocks on this list.

At a D grade, the stock is considered expensive relative to its fundamentals even after Friday's beat, while sector peers Colgate-Palmolive (CL) fares even worse with a D-, and Church & Dwight (CHD) and WD-40 Company (WDFC) both sit at the bottom with F grades, the most overvalued readings on the entire list. The read-through peers that moved higher alongside P&G on Friday, CHD, CLX, and CL, occupy very different positions on the valuation spectrum.

At the other end of the scale, Clorox (CLX) and Reynolds Consumer Products (REYN) stand out as the best-valued names in the sector, both earning B grades — the highest on the list and squarely in Buy territory. Central Garden & Pet (CENT) follows with a B-, rounding out the three names where valuation grades suggest the stock is reasonably priced relative to fundamentals. Kimberly-Clark (KMB) sits in the middle with a C+, representing a more neutral valuation picture.

The valuation grade reflects how expensive a stock is relative to its fundamentals. A grade of B- or above is considered a Buy signal, while D+ or below indicates a Sell, and F represents the most overvalued end of the spectrum.

Here is the list:

  1. The Clorox Company (CLX), Valuation grade: B

  2. Reynolds Consumer Products Inc. (REYN), Valuation grade: B

  3. Central Garden & Pet Company (CENT), Valuation grade: B-

  4. Kimberly-Clark Corporation (KMB), Valuation grade: C+

  5. The Procter & Gamble Company (PG), Valuation grade: D

  6. Colgate-Palmolive Company (CL), Valuation grade: D-

  7. Church & Dwight Co., Inc. (CHD), Valuation grade: F

  8. WD-40 Company (WDFC), Valuation grade: F
